events.title: September 11 Attacks — 2,977 Killed in Coordinated Al-Qaeda Terror Strikes
events.detail: Al-Qaeda operatives hijacked four commercial aircraft. Two destroyed the World Trade Center towers in New York City, one struck the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia, and one crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania after passengers intervened. The deadliest attack on US soil, triggering the Global War on Terror.

events.title: USA PATRIOT Act Signed — Sweeping Surveillance Authority Expansion
events.detail: President Bush signed the PATRIOT Act on October 26, 2001, dramatically expanding federal authority to conduct surveillance on phone calls, emails, financial records, and conduct 'sneak and peek' searches with delayed notification. The law passed with near-unanimous support in Congress in the weeks after 9/11. Civil liberties groups from both left and right raised concerns about its potential for abuse, concerns later vindicated by the 2013 Snowden disclosures.

media.source: Wikipedia / Mass surveillance in the United States

events.title: Operation Enduring Freedom — US Invades Afghanistan; Taliban Falls in 9 Weeks
events.detail: The United States and coalition partners launched air strikes and ground operations in Afghanistan on October 7, 2001, targeting Taliban government forces and al-Qaeda training camps. The Taliban regime collapsed by December 2001. However, Osama bin Laden escaped at Tora Bora in December 2001, and the war would last 20 years — America's longest, with over 2,400 US troops killed and $2.3 trillion spent before the 2021 withdrawal.

events.title: Anthrax Letter Attacks — 5 Killed, 17 Infected; Congressional Offices Evacuated
events.detail: Letters containing weaponized anthrax spores were mailed to news media offices and Democratic senators in September–October 2001, killing five people and infecting 17 others. The attacks shut down congressional offices, infected the Brentwood postal facility in DC, and intensified post-9/11 fears of bioterrorism. After a seven-year investigation, the FBI concluded Army biodefense researcher Bruce Ivins was responsible; Ivins died by suicide in July 2008 before charges were filed.

media.source: Wikipedia / Prelude to the Iraq War

events.title: Department of Homeland Security Created — Largest Government Reorganization Since 1947
events.detail: The Homeland Security Act of 2002 created the Department of Homeland Security, merging 22 federal agencies and over 187,000 employees — the largest US government reorganization since the National Security Act of 1947. The DHS consolidated CBP, ICE, FEMA, TSA, the Secret Service, and Coast Guard under a single cabinet department. The 9/11 Commission had identified communications failures between agencies as a key factor enabling the attacks.

events.title: Space Shuttle Columbia Disaster — 7 Astronauts Lost on Re-Entry
events.detail: Space Shuttle Columbia disintegrated during atmospheric re-entry on February 1, 2003, killing all seven crew members — including the first Israeli astronaut, Ilan Ramon. The cause was a foam insulation strike on the left wing during launch 16 days earlier that damaged thermal protection tiles. The accident led to a 29-month stand-down of shuttle flights, accelerated the program's eventual retirement in 2011, and prompted sweeping changes to NASA safety culture.

events.title: US-Led Invasion of Iraq — Saddam Falls; No WMD Found
events.detail: The United States, United Kingdom, and coalition partners invaded Iraq on March 20, 2003, citing weapons of mass destruction that were never found. Baghdad fell in three weeks; Saddam Hussein was captured in December 2003. The subsequent insurgency and sectarian civil war killed 4,487 US service members and over 100,000 Iraqi civilians. The war cost an estimated $2 trillion, destabilized the Middle East, and created the conditions that gave rise to ISIS.

events.title: Abu Ghraib Prisoner Abuse — Photos Reveal Systematic Torture of Iraqi Detainees
events.detail: CBS News and The New Yorker published photographs in April 2004 showing US military personnel at Abu Ghraib prison humiliating, sexually abusing, and torturing Iraqi detainees — including use of stress positions, attack dogs, sleep deprivation, and simulated drowning. The images shocked the world and severely damaged America's international credibility. Investigations led to 11 lower-ranking soldiers being convicted; no senior officers were charged. Defense Secretary Rumsfeld survived calls for his resignation.

events.title: Hurricane Katrina — 1,833 Killed; Levee Failures Flood 80% of New Orleans
events.detail: Hurricane Katrina made landfall on the Gulf Coast on August 29, 2005 as a Category 4 storm. New Orleans levee failures flooded 80% of the city; over 1,833 people died and more than one million were displaced. The federal government's catastrophically slow response — FEMA Director Michael Brown's 'Brownie, you're doing a heckuva job' moment — became a defining failure of the Bush administration, exposing deep racial and economic inequalities in disaster preparedness and federal emergency management.

events.title: NSA Warrantless Wiretapping Revealed — Bush Secretly Bypassed FISA Courts Since 2001
events.detail: The New York Times revealed in December 2005 that President Bush had secretly authorized the NSA to wiretap Americans' international communications without court approval, bypassing the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA). The program, authorized days after 9/11, was defended by Bush as a vital antiterror tool. The revelation triggered congressional investigations and an eventual FISA Amendments Act (2008) legalizing some warrantless collection — a controversy reignited by Snowden in 2013.

events.title: Iraq Surge — 21,500 Additional Troops; Petraeus Counterinsurgency Doctrine
events.detail: President Bush announced a 'surge' of 21,500 additional US troops to Iraq in January 2007, adopting a counterinsurgency strategy developed by General David Petraeus. Combined with the 'Sunni Awakening' — Sunni tribal militias turning against al-Qaeda — the surge significantly reduced violence by 2008. Petraeus testified to Congress that the strategy was working; critics warned it delayed rather than resolved Iraq's underlying political failures. The surge became the template for future US counterinsurgency doctrine.

events.title: 2008 Financial Crisis — Lehman Brothers Collapses; $700B TARP Bailout Passed
events.detail: Lehman Brothers filed for the largest bankruptcy in US history on September 15, 2008, triggering a global financial panic. The government bailed out AIG ($182B), nationalized Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, and Congress passed the $700 billion TARP bank rescue. US unemployment surged to 10% by October 2009; GDP contracted 4.3% from peak to trough — the worst recession since the Great Depression. An estimated $11 trillion in household wealth was destroyed.

events.title: Obama Inaugurated — First African American President; 1.8 Million at National Mall
events.detail: Barack Obama was inaugurated as the 44th President on January 20, 2009, becoming the first African American to hold the office. An estimated 1.8 million people attended the National Mall ceremony. Obama inherited two wars, a collapsing economy (-5.4% GDP growth in Q4 2008), and an unemployment rate rising toward 10%. He entered office with a 67% approval rating and a Democratic supermajority in the Senate.

events.title: American Recovery and Reinvestment Act — $787B Stimulus Signed
events.detail: President Obama signed the ARRA on February 17, 2009 — the largest stimulus in US history to that point. The $787B package included $288B in tax cuts, $144B for state/local relief, and $357B in infrastructure, education, and healthcare spending. CBO later found it created or saved 3.3 million jobs at its peak in 2010. Unemployment still peaked at 10% in October 2009 but economists generally credited the ARRA with shortening the recession.

events.title: Affordable Care Act Signed — Most Significant US Healthcare Expansion Since Medicare (1965)
events.detail: President Obama signed the Affordable Care Act on March 23, 2010, establishing insurance exchanges, expanding Medicaid, mandating coverage of pre-existing conditions, allowing dependents to remain on parents' plans until 26, and requiring most Americans to carry insurance. By 2016, approximately 20 million previously uninsured Americans gained coverage. Not a single Republican voted for the law. The Supreme Court upheld it 5-4 in 2012 (NFIB v. Sebelius) and 7-2 in 2021 (California v. Texas).

events.title: Osama bin Laden Killed — SEAL Team Six Operation Neptune Spear in Abbottabad
events.detail: US Navy SEAL Team Six killed Osama bin Laden in a 40-minute raid on his compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan on May 2, 2011 — nearly 10 years after the 9/11 attacks he masterminded. President Obama authorized the operation against advice from some senior officials. Bin Laden was shot and killed; his body was buried at sea. The operation was conducted without Pakistan's knowledge, creating lasting diplomatic tensions. Crowds gathered at the White House and Ground Zero to celebrate.

events.title: US Withdraws from Iraq — 8-Year War Ends; 4,487 US Troops Killed
events.detail: The last US combat troops crossed from Iraq into Kuwait on December 18, 2011, formally ending the 8-year Iraq War. A total of 4,487 US troops were killed in action and 32,226 were wounded. Over 100,000 Iraqi civilians died by various estimates. The war cost an estimated $800B directly; long-term costs including veterans' care exceeded $2 trillion. ISIS's rise in 2014 forced the US to return with a smaller advisory and air-power mission under Operation Inherent Resolve.

events.title: Sandy Hook Shooting — 20 Children, 6 Adults Killed; Senate Background Check Bill Fails
events.detail: A gunman killed 20 first-graders and 6 school staff at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Connecticut on December 14, 2012. President Obama called it the worst day of his presidency. A Senate bill expanding background checks for gun sales failed in April 2013 despite 90% public support, blocked by a Republican filibuster. Alex Jones's years of false claims that Sandy Hook was staged resulted in $1.5B in defamation judgments against him in 2022.

media.source: Wikipedia / Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting

events.title: Boston Marathon Bombing — 3 Killed, 264 Injured; City Locked Down for 4 Days
events.detail: Chechen-American brothers Dzhokhar and Tamerlan Tsarnaev detonated two homemade pressure cooker bombs near the Boston Marathon finish line on April 15, 2013, killing 3 and injuring 264. Tamerlan was killed in a police confrontation three days later; Dzhokhar was captured after a massive manhunt that locked down the Boston metropolitan area. Dzhokhar Tsarnaev was convicted on all 30 counts in 2015 and sentenced to death — a sentence upheld by the Supreme Court in 2022.

events.title: Snowden Reveals NSA Mass Surveillance — PRISM, Bulk Phone Metadata, XKeyscore
events.detail: Former NSA contractor Edward Snowden disclosed to The Guardian and Washington Post in June 2013 a series of classified mass surveillance programs: PRISM (bulk collection of data from Google, Facebook, Apple, and other tech giants); a secret FISA Court order requiring Verizon to hand over all call metadata daily; and XKeyscore, enabling near-real-time search of global internet traffic. The revelations triggered global outrage, reforms to Section 215 of the PATRIOT Act, and US-allied diplomatic crises. Snowden fled to Russia where he received asylum and subsequently citizenship.

events.title: ISIS Declares Caliphate — Captures Mosul; Controls UK-Sized Territory in Iraq and Syria
events.detail: ISIS (Islamic State/ISIL/Daesh) captured Mosul, Iraq's second-largest city, in June 2014 and declared a 'caliphate' spanning Iraq and Syria. At its peak, ISIS controlled territory the size of the United Kingdom with 8 million people, generating $1–2B annually from oil, extortion, and looting. President Obama launched Operation Inherent Resolve in August 2014, building a 60-nation coalition. The physical caliphate was declared defeated in March 2019; ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi was killed in a US raid in October 2019.

events.title: Obergefell v. Hodges — Supreme Court Legalizes Same-Sex Marriage Nationwide
events.detail: The Supreme Court ruled 5-4 in Obergefell v. Hodges that the Constitution's due process and equal protection clauses require all states to license and recognize same-sex marriages. Justice Anthony Kennedy wrote the majority opinion. The ruling instantly legalized same-sex marriage in all 50 states. The White House was illuminated in rainbow colors. A decade earlier, 11 states had passed constitutional amendments banning same-sex marriage on the same day in 2004.

media.source: Wikipedia / Legal status of same-sex marriage

events.title: San Bernardino Terror Attack — 14 Killed; FBI-Apple Encryption Battle Follows
events.detail: Syed Rizwan Farook and Tashfeen Malik opened fire at a San Bernardino County holiday party on December 2, 2015, killing 14 and wounding 22. Malik had pledged allegiance to ISIS on Facebook during the attack. Both attackers were killed in a police confrontation 4 hours later. The attack triggered a landmark legal battle when the FBI sought Apple's help unlocking Farook's iPhone — Apple refused, arguing the precedent threatened all users' security. The FBI eventually paid over $1M to an outside firm to crack the phone.

events.title: Trump First Inauguration — 'America First' Era Begins
events.detail: Donald Trump was inaugurated as the 45th President on January 20, 2017, delivering a bleak 'American carnage' address. He immediately pursued an 'America First' agenda: withdrawing from the Trans-Pacific Partnership and Paris Climate Accord, imposing steel and aluminum tariffs, challenging NATO burden-sharing, and beginning a trade war with China. Trump's first term was marked by extraordinary volatility — two impeachments, a pandemic, and the January 6 Capitol attack.

media.source: Wikipedia / First inauguration of Donald Trump

events.title: Trump Travel Ban — EO 13769 Blocks 7 Muslim-Majority Nations; Airport Protests Erupt
events.detail: Trump signed Executive Order 13769 on January 27, 2017 — his seventh day in office — suspending entry from Iraq, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, and Yemen for 90 days, halting the refugee program, and indefinitely banning Syrian refugees. Immediate chaos erupted at airports as green card holders and visa holders were detained. Federal courts rapidly blocked the order; after two revisions, a modified version was upheld 5-4 by the Supreme Court in Trump v. Hawaii (2018).

events.title: Charlottesville 'Unite the Right' — Heather Heyer Killed; Trump's 'Very Fine People' Remarks
events.detail: White supremacist and neo-Nazi groups converged on Charlottesville, Virginia on August 11–12, 2017, ostensibly to protest the removal of a Robert E. Lee statue. Counter-protester Heather Heyer was killed when James Alex Fields Jr. drove his car into the crowd, injuring 35 others. Trump's statement that there were 'very fine people on both sides' — including the white nationalists — drew widespread condemnation. Fields was sentenced to life in prison plus 419 years for federal hate crimes.

events.title: Las Vegas Mass Shooting — 60 Killed, 411 Shot; Deadliest Modern US Mass Shooting
events.detail: Stephen Paddock fired from the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel into the crowd at the Route 91 Harvest country music festival on October 1, 2017, killing 60 and injuring 411 — the deadliest mass shooting in modern US history. Paddock had modified 14 semi-automatic rifles with 'bump stocks' to simulate automatic fire. The FBI closed the investigation without establishing a motive; bump stocks were subsequently banned by the Trump administration in 2018.

events.title: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act — Corporate Rate Cut 35%→21%; Largest Tax Overhaul Since 1986
events.detail: Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act on December 22, 2017, reducing the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21%, temporarily cutting individual income tax rates, doubling the standard deduction, and limiting SALT deductions to $10,000. The CBO estimated it would add $1.9 trillion to the national debt over 10 years. GDP grew 2.9% in 2018; critics noted most benefits accrued to corporations and top earners, while the law's individual provisions were set to expire in 2025.

media.source: Wikipedia / Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

events.title: Mueller Report Released — Russia Interference Confirmed; 10 Obstruction Instances Documented
events.detail: Special Counsel Robert Mueller's 448-page report, released publicly on April 18, 2019, found that Russia had systematically interfered in the 2016 election 'in sweeping and systematic fashion' to benefit Trump, but did not establish a criminal conspiracy between the Trump campaign and Russia. The report documented 10 potential instances of obstruction of justice by Trump, but Mueller declined to charge a sitting president per longstanding DOJ policy. AG Barr's summary characterization was later found by a judge to be misleading.

events.title: First Trump Impeachment — Abuse of Power, Ukraine Quid Pro Quo; Senate Acquits
events.detail: The House of Representatives impeached Trump 230-197 on December 18, 2019 on charges of abuse of power and obstruction of Congress — the third presidential impeachment in US history. The charges stemmed from a July 25, 2019 phone call in which Trump allegedly conditioned congressionally approved military aid to Ukraine on a political investigation into Joe Biden. The Senate acquitted Trump along nearly party-line votes on February 5, 2020.

media.source: Wikipedia / First impeachment trial of Donald Trump

events.title: COVID-19 National Emergency — 1.2 Million Americans Die in Deadliest US Epidemic Since 1918
events.detail: Trump declared a national emergency on March 13, 2020 as COVID-19 spread across the US. The pandemic ultimately killed over 1.2 million Americans — the deadliest epidemic in US history since the 1918 influenza. GDP contracted 19.2% (annualized) in Q2 2020. Operation Warp Speed produced two authorized mRNA vaccines within 12 months — unprecedented in medical history. The $2.2T CARES Act and subsequent legislation provided over $5 trillion in pandemic relief.

media.source: Wikipedia / List of epidemics and pandemics

events.title: George Floyd Killed — Largest US Protest Movement Since 1960s; 15–26 Million March
events.detail: Minneapolis police officer Derek Chauvin knelt on George Floyd's neck for 9 minutes and 29 seconds on May 25, 2020, killing him. The murder, captured on video and witnessed globally, triggered an estimated 15–26 million Americans participating in Black Lives Matter protests — the largest protest movement in US history. Parts of Minneapolis burned; Confederate and colonial statues were toppled nationwide. Chauvin was convicted of second-degree murder in April 2021 and sentenced to 22.5 years.

events.title: Capitol Riot — Trump Supporters Storm Congress; Certification Delayed; 140 Officers Injured
events.detail: Hundreds of Trump supporters stormed the US Capitol on January 6, 2021, attempting to prevent Congress from certifying Biden's 2020 Electoral College victory. The assault injured 140 police officers and resulted in five deaths during or immediately after the event. Trump was impeached a second time 57-43 in the Senate for 'incitement of insurrection' — below the 2/3 threshold for conviction. Over 1,265 people were eventually charged. The January 6th House Select Committee's 845-page report found Trump criminally liable for the attack.

events.title: Biden Inaugurated — 25,000 National Guard Deployed After January 6 Insurrection
events.detail: Joseph R. Biden was inaugurated as the 46th President on January 20, 2021 — two weeks after the Capitol riot. The National Mall was empty of spectators but lined with 25,000 National Guard troops and temporary security fencing. Biden immediately reversed dozens of Trump executive orders, rejoining the Paris Climate Agreement, WHO, and pledging to 'rebuild alliances.' He entered office with 57% approval and a historic $1.9 trillion COVID relief bill already moving through Congress.

media.source: Wikipedia / January 6 United States Capitol attack

events.title: American Rescue Plan — $1.9T COVID Relief Cuts Child Poverty 40%, Sends $1,400 Checks
events.detail: President Biden signed the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan on March 11, 2021, including $1,400 direct payments to most Americans, an expanded Child Tax Credit that cut child poverty by approximately 40%, $350B for state and local governments, and $160B for vaccines and testing. The plan passed on pure party-line votes. Critics including Larry Summers warned it was inflationary; supporters credited it with the fastest labor market recovery since World War II.

media.source: Wikipedia / American Rescue Plan Act of 2021

events.title: US Withdraws from Afghanistan — Taliban Seizes Kabul in 11 Days; 13 Marines Killed at Abbey Gate
events.detail: US forces completed their withdrawal from Afghanistan on August 30, 2021, ending America's longest war (19 years, 10 months). The Taliban captured all provincial capitals and entered Kabul within 11 days of the final withdrawal beginning, collapsing the US-backed government. A chaotic airlift at Kabul airport evacuated 124,000 civilians. An ISIS-K suicide bomber killed 13 US service members and 170 Afghans at Abbey Gate on August 26. Total American losses: 2,461 killed, 20,744 wounded, $2.3 trillion spent.

events.title: Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act — $1.2T Bipartisan Package for Roads, Rail, Broadband
events.detail: Biden signed the bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act on November 15, 2021, committing $1.2 trillion over 10 years to road and bridge repair, rail modernization, broadband internet expansion, water system upgrades, and electrical grid resilience. The bill passed 69-30 in the Senate with 19 Republican votes — a rare bipartisan achievement. It was the largest US infrastructure investment since Eisenhower's Interstate Highway System in the 1950s.

events.title: US Inflation Hits 9.1% — 41-Year High; Fed Launches Fastest Rate Hike Cycle Since 1980s
events.detail: The US Consumer Price Index reached 9.1% year-over-year in June 2022 — the highest since November 1981 — driven by pandemic supply chain disruptions, Russia's invasion of Ukraine (which spiked energy prices), and pandemic-era fiscal stimulus. The Federal Reserve responded with 11 interest rate hikes from March 2022 to July 2023, raising the federal funds rate from near-zero to 5.25–5.5% — the most aggressive tightening since the Volcker era. Inflation fell to 3.1% by early 2024.

events.title: Uvalde School Shooting — 19 Children, 2 Teachers Killed; Police Delayed 77 Minutes
events.detail: An 18-year-old gunman killed 19 children and 2 teachers at Robb Elementary School in Uvalde, Texas on May 24, 2022, using an AR-15 purchased legally on his 18th birthday. The attack was the deadliest school shooting since Sandy Hook. A Texas House investigation found police waited 77 minutes before breaching the classroom — a catastrophic failure documented by security footage. The Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, including enhanced background checks for under-21 buyers, passed Congress in June 2022.

events.title: Dobbs v. Jackson — Supreme Court Overturns Roe v. Wade, Eliminating Federal Abortion Right
events.detail: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization on June 24, 2022, overturning Roe v. Wade (1973) and eliminating the federal constitutional right to abortion — the first time the Court had eliminated a previously recognized fundamental right. The draft opinion had leaked in May 2022, prompting unprecedented protests. Within months, 13 states enacted near-total abortion bans. Democrats made abortion access a central issue in the 2022 and 2024 elections.

events.title: Silicon Valley Bank Collapses — Largest US Bank Failure Since 2008; FDIC Backstops Deposits
events.detail: Silicon Valley Bank, the 16th-largest US bank and primary lender to tech startups, collapsed on March 10, 2023 after a bank run triggered by interest rate losses on its bond portfolio. Signature Bank failed two days later. The FDIC, Treasury, and Fed jointly backstopped all deposits above the $250,000 FDIC limit to prevent broader contagion. First Republic Bank then failed in May 2023 and was acquired by JPMorgan — the third major US bank failure in two months, the most turbulent stretch for US banking since 2008.

events.title: Maui Wildfires — 102 Killed in Lahaina; Deadliest US Wildfire Disaster in Over a Century
events.detail: Wind-driven wildfires fueled by Hurricane Dora destroyed the historic town of Lahaina on the island of Maui, Hawaii on August 8, 2023, killing at least 102 people — the deadliest US wildfire disaster since the 1918 Cloquet Fire in Minnesota. Over 2,200 structures were destroyed. Questions arose about the delayed activation of emergency sirens, utility liability for downed power lines, and climate change's role in intensifying wildfire risk. A $4B settlement with Hawaiian Electric was reached in 2024.

events.title: Hamas Oct. 7 Attack — 1,200 Israelis Killed; US Deploys Two Carrier Strike Groups to Eastern Med
events.detail: Hamas launched a devastating assault from Gaza on October 7, 2023, killing approximately 1,200 Israelis and taking 251 hostages — the deadliest day for Jews since the Holocaust. The US immediately deployed two aircraft carrier strike groups to the Eastern Mediterranean as a show of force, pledging ironclad support for Israel. Israel's subsequent military campaign in Gaza killed over 35,000 Palestinians according to UN figures by mid-2024, triggering a humanitarian crisis, campus protests across the US, and deep divisions within the Democratic Party.

events.title: Trump Wins 2024 Election — Returns as 47th President After Historic Felony Conviction
events.detail: Donald Trump won the 2024 presidential election, defeating Democratic nominee Kamala Harris with 312 electoral votes to her 226. Trump became the first convicted felon elected president, having been convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records in May 2024. Biden withdrew from the race in July 2024, endorsing Vice President Harris. Republicans swept the Senate and retained the House, giving Trump a governing trifecta. Trump received more popular votes than in 2020 (77.2M vs. 74.2M).

media.source: Wikipedia / Second presidency of Donald Trump

events.title: Trump Second Inauguration — Day-One Executive Actions
events.detail: Donald Trump inaugurated for a second term. Signed sweeping executive orders on immigration enforcement, energy, DEI programs, and federal workforce on Day One. Declared a national emergency at the southern border.

media.source: Wikipedia / List of executive orders in the second Trump presidency

events.title: Southern Border National Emergency — Military Deployed; Title 8 Enforcement Maximized
events.detail: On his first day, Trump declared a national emergency at the southern border, invoking Title 8 expedited removal authorities and deploying active-duty military personnel to assist CBP. The administration reinstated 'Remain in Mexico' (MPP), suspended asylum processing, and ended CBP One app appointments for migrants. Encounters at the southern border dropped dramatically in early 2025 as enforcement intensified and smuggling networks adapted. The military deployment drew scrutiny from legal experts citing the Posse Comitatus Act.

events.title: Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) Launched Under Elon Musk
events.detail: The Department of Government Efficiency, an advisory body led by Elon Musk, began operations with a stated mission to cut federal spending. DOGE proceeded to eliminate federal jobs, cancel contracts, and shut programs, claiming over $160B in savings by April 2026 — a figure disputed by independent analysts who estimated $135B in associated disruption costs. A CNN investigation in April 2026 found DOGE cuts hampered US capabilities in Iran-war readiness, terror monitoring, and cybersecurity at the worst possible time.

events.title: 'Liberation Day' Tariffs Announced — Sweeping Reciprocal Duties
events.detail: Trump announced sweeping reciprocal tariffs targeting nearly all US trading partners on what the White House called 'Liberation Day.' Rates ranged from 10% to over 100% on specific country/product combinations. Financial markets experienced severe volatility; some tariffs were subsequently paused or modified via bilateral deals.

events.title: ICE FY2025: 442,637 Formal Deportations — Record High; 70% Have No Criminal Conviction
events.detail: ICE deported 442,637 people in Fiscal Year 2025 — a record high released officially on April 15, 2026. The data showed approximately 70.8% of deportees had no criminal conviction. Total departures (formal deportations plus self-departures under pressure) exceeded 2.5 million since January 2025. ICE set a 1-million-per-year deportation target for FY2026, requiring the Senate's $70B budget reconciliation passed April 23, 2026.

events.title: Executive Order on Voter Citizenship Verification and Mail Ballot Restrictions
events.detail: Trump signed an EO directing DHS and SSA to compile a national voter citizenship list, and restricting mail ballots to verified citizens. Immediately challenged as unconstitutional by Democratic AGs and voting rights groups.

events.title: Artemis II — Successful Splashdown Completes First Crewed Lunar Mission Since Apollo 17
events.detail: NASA launched four astronauts on Artemis II from Kennedy Space Center on April 1, 2026. The crew completed a lunar flyby on April 6 — the first humans near the Moon since Apollo 17 in December 1972 — setting a crewed distance record of 252,756 miles from Earth. Mission Control declared a 'perfect bullseye splashdown' at 5:07 p.m. PDT on April 10 off San Diego. Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialist Christina Koch (NASA), and Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen (CSA) were recovered by USS John P. Murtha, marking a historic milestone on the path to Artemis III's crewed Moon landing.

events.title: Section 232 Steel/Aluminum/Copper Tariffs Expanded to 50% Flat Rate
events.detail: Trump restructured Section 232 duties to apply a flat 50% tariff on the full customs value of steel, aluminum, copper, and derivative products, effective April 6, 2026. Simultaneously imposed up to 100% tariffs on patented pharmaceutical imports. The tariff escalation triggered a sharp equity market selloff with the S&P 500 and Dow each falling 9–10% — analysts comparing the moves to the 1987 Black Monday event.

events.title: Trump Cites SCOTUS Immunity Ruling in ~1/3 of Emergency Court Filings
events.detail: An ABC News review published April 7, 2026 found that the Trump administration cited the Supreme Court's 2024 Trump v. United States immunity ruling in nearly a third of its 29 emergency SCOTUS applications in the second term — across federal firings, immigration, trade, and military authority. Legal scholars warned of expansive constitutional precedent-setting beyond the ruling's original criminal prosecution context.

events.title: Trump-Iran Ceasefire Collapses — Vance Walks Out of Islamabad Talks, Hormuz Blockade Ordered
events.detail: President Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran on April 8, 2026, with Iran agreeing to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. VP Vance led the US delegation in direct face-to-face talks with Iran in Islamabad on April 11-12 — the first such engagement since 2015 — but the talks collapsed after 21 hours without agreement on April 12. Iran rejected US demands for a nuclear non-proliferation commitment; the US rejected Iranian demands for Hormuz toll rights, reparations, and asset release. Trump immediately ordered a US Navy blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, and WTI crude surged nearly 6% to ~$105/barrel.

events.title: US Hormuz Blockade: 10,000+ Troops Deployed; Iran Blocks Tankers; Ceasefire Expires Apr 21
events.detail: The US naval blockade of Iranian ports took formal effect April 13, 2026, deploying 10,000+ troops and 12+ warships. By April 16, 13-14 ships had been turned away without boarding. The S&P 500 paradoxically hit a record 7,022.95 on April 15 on peace deal optimism. Iran FM Araghchi briefly declared the Strait open on April 17 before Parliamentary Speaker Ghalibaf reversed it. On April 18, Iran reimposed 'strict control' and IRGC gunboats fired on an Indian-flagged tanker. Trump dispatched Kushner and Witkoff to Islamabad for new talks with ceasefire set to expire April 21.

events.title: Trump Signs EO Fast-Tracking Psychedelic Research for Veterans (Psilocybin, Ibogaine, MDMA)
events.detail: Trump signed an executive order April 17, 2026 directing the FDA to fast-track reviews for psychedelic drugs including psilocybin, ibogaine, LSD, and MDMA — targeting veterans with PTSD and depression. HHS Secretary RFK Jr. and Joe Rogan attended the Oval Office signing. The FDA simultaneously cleared the first ibogaine IND application. DEA and DOJ were directed to initiate Schedule I rescheduling reviews for substances completing Phase 3 trials.

events.title: S&P 500 Hits 7,000 for First Time — Record High Amid Iran War
events.detail: The S&P 500 closed at 7,022.95 on April 15, 2026, crossing 7,000 for the first time in history. The Nasdaq recorded its 12th consecutive gain — longest win streak since 2009. The rally came despite the US naval blockade of Iran, driven by AI/tech stocks and peace deal optimism. The index had fallen 9-10% on April 6 tariff escalation, then fully recovered to record highs within 10 days.

events.title: Trump Extends Ceasefire Indefinitely — IRGC Seizes Two Hormuz Ships; Diplomatic Stalemate Deepens
events.detail: After the ceasefire expired April 21 with no deal, Trump announced via Truth Social on April 22 that he was extending the truce indefinitely 'at Pakistan's request,' keeping the US naval blockade fully in place while claiming Iran was losing '$500 million a day' and 'collapsing financially.' On the same day, Iran's IRGC Navy seized two container ships in the Strait of Hormuz — the MSC Francesca and the Epaminondas — and fired on three additional vessels, marking the most significant maritime escalation since the blockade began April 13.

events.title: DOJ Reclassifies Medical Marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III — Major Federal Drug Policy Shift
events.detail: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche signed an order on April 23, 2026 reclassifying state-licensed and FDA-approved medical marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III under the Controlled Substances Act — the most significant federal marijuana policy change since the drug was listed under Schedule I in 1970. The reclassification exempts cannabis companies from IRS Section 280E tax provisions (saving the industry ~$2B/year), expands research pathways, and opens banking access. Recreational marijuana remains federally illegal.

events.title: S&P 500 Hits New All-Time High of 7,165 — AI Mega-Caps Lead; Analyst Median Target 7,650
events.detail: The S&P 500 set a new all-time high of 7,165.08 on April 24, 2026, surpassing the April 15 record of 7,022.95. The rally was led by AI mega-caps — Nvidia, Microsoft, and Meta — along with Intel's strong quarterly earnings. Year-end Wall Street analyst median target stood at 7,650. Consumer sentiment remained low (UMich final April: 49.8) amid Iran war oil prices and tariff-era cost increases.

events.title: WHCD Shooting — Gunman Cole Allen Arrested; Trump Evacuated; One Officer Shot
events.detail: A gunman opened fire outside the White House Correspondents' Dinner at the Washington Hilton on April 25–26, 2026, prompting the evacuation of President Trump, Vice President Vance, and Cabinet members. One law enforcement officer was shot but survived thanks to body armor. Suspect Cole Allen, 31, was arrested at the scene. Investigators said Allen appeared to target Trump administration officials. The incident prompted an immediate review of WHCD security protocols.

media.source: Wikipedia / 2026 White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting

events.title: Islamabad Iran Talks Collapse — Araghchi Leaves Pakistan; Trump Cancels Witkoff Mission
events.detail: The Islamabad diplomatic channel for US-Iran negotiations collapsed on April 26, 2026, as Iranian FM Araghchi left Pakistan and Trump canceled the planned Witkoff/Kushner diplomatic mission, citing 'time-wasting.' Iran's FM said there would be no 'forced negotiations'; Trump said Iran 'offered lot, but not enough.' WTI crude held near $95–97/bbl as the Hormuz blockade continued on day 58 of the ceasefire. FISA Section 702 reauthorization faced a House vote with just 4 days to the April 30 expiry.
